The estimates are not exactly equal to truth because the panel is finite. The validation target is recovery within strict tolerances, not exact equality in one finite sample.

\subsection{Counterfactual Recovery}
\begin{table}[H]
\centering\small
\caption{Counterfactual types.}
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\toprule
Type & Intervention & Purpose \\
\midrule
Type A & Shift reward, hold transitions fixed & Payoff counterfactual \\
Type B & Change transitions, hold reward fixed & State-dynamics counterfactual \\
Type C & Disable one non-anchor action & Action-set/design counterfactual \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}[H]
\centering\small
\caption{Counterfactual recovery against known oracle objects.}
\begin{tabular}{lrrrr}
\toprule
Counterfactual & Policy TV & Policy KL & Value RMSE & Regret \\
\midrule
Type A & 0.005109 & $7.56\times 10^{-5}$ & 0.000238 & 0.000213 \\
Type B & 0.005457 & $8.20\times 10^{-5}$ & 0.000363 & 0.000362 \\
Type C & 0.003548 & $3.56\times 10^{-5}$ & 0.000114 & 0.000086 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

These regrets are small because the recovered reward is close enough to the true reward that re-solving the intervened model produces almost the same policy as the oracle.
